question: Q1
title: SSRF via user-controlled chatbot training data URL
data_flow: config 'application.chatBot.trainingData' -> isUrl() check -> download(trainingFile) -> fs.writeFile. If the config value is attacker-controlled via environment variable injection or config file tampering, the server fetches an arbitrary URL server-side and writes the response body to disk.
exploit_scenario: Attacker controls the chatBot.trainingData config (e.g., env var override in a misconfigured deployment). They set it to 'http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/'. The `download` npm package fetches it server-side, the response is written to 'data/chatbot/<filename>', then parsed via JSON.parse — leaking cloud metadata credentials or internal service responses.
framework_protection: None. utils.isUrl() only gates whether to download; it does not restrict URL scheme or target host. No allowlist of safe domains is enforced.
fix_recommendation: Validate the URL against a strict allowlist of trusted hostnames. Block RFC-1918, loopback, link-local, and cloud metadata IP ranges. Use a sandboxed HTTP client with SSRF mitigations (e.g., ssrf-req-filter npm package). Prefer bundling training data statically rather than fetching at runtime.

question: Q3
title: RCE via vm.runInContext evaluating user-supplied orderLinesData
exact_code:
const orderLinesData = body.orderLinesData || ''
const sandbox = { safeEval, orderLinesData }
vm.createContext(sandbox)
vm.runInContext('safeEval(orderLinesData)', sandbox, { timeout: 2000 })
data_flow: HTTP POST body.orderLinesData -> vm sandbox context -> vm.runInContext evaluates safeEval(orderLinesData). The user-controlled string is the expression being evaluated inside the notevil wrapper.
exploit_scenario: Attacker POSTs a crafted payload with orderLinesData containing a notevil sandbox escape via constructor chain. Node.js vm module explicitly documents it is NOT a security boundary. Known vm escapes via __proto__ or constructor.constructor yield arbitrary code execution in the server process.
framework_protection: Partial: notevil wraps eval to restrict dangerous operations; vm.createContext provides some isolation. However, the Node.js vm module is not a security sandbox — escapes are well-documented. The 2000ms timeout only limits DoS, not code execution.
fix_recommendation: Remove user-controlled eval entirely. If expression evaluation is required, parse and evaluate only a strict whitelist of allowed arithmetic/logic operations using a purpose-built AST evaluator. Never pass raw user input to vm.runInContext or any eval-like function.

question: Q3
title: XXE with SSRF potential via user-uploaded XML parsed with noent:true
data_flow: Uploaded .xml file buffer -> toString() -> libxmljs2.parseXml() with noent:true. The noent flag enables XML external entity expansion. User controls the full XML document including DOCTYPE and entity declarations.
exploit_scenario: Attacker uploads XML containing an external entity declaration referencing 'file:///etc/passwd' or an internal network URL such as 'http://internal-service:8080/admin'. The noent:true flag causes libxmljs2 to resolve the entity and embed the target content. The parsed document content is reflected in the error response, disclosing the data. The vm wrapper does not prevent this since XXE resolution occurs inside the libxml C library, below the JS sandbox layer.
framework_protection: The vm timeout limits DoS-style attacks. The challengeUtils.solveIf calls confirm this is intentional, but the XXE is fully exploitable against real infrastructure. No scheme or host restrictions are applied to entity URLs.
fix_recommendation: Disable external entity expansion: remove 'noent: true' or add 'nonet: true' to block network entities. Consider switching to a JS-native parser (xml2js) with entity expansion disabled. Never reflect parsed user-supplied XML content in error messages.

question: Q2
title: Unsafe YAML deserialization via yaml.load on user-uploaded file content
data_flow: Uploaded .yml/.yaml file -> buffer.toString() -> yaml.load(data) inside vm context. js-yaml yaml.load() with the default FULL_SCHEMA can instantiate arbitrary JS objects via YAML type tags or enable prototype pollution via crafted keys.
exploit_scenario: In js-yaml versions supporting FULL_SCHEMA, uploading a YAML file with a !!js/function tag causes function evaluation during deserialization — leading to arbitrary code execution. In js-yaml 4.x, prototype pollution via crafted YAML object keys (e.g., __proto__ mapped to {admin: true}) can corrupt the prototype chain if downstream code merges the parsed object without hasOwnProperty guards. YAML bomb payloads trigger the DoS path.
framework_protection: The vm timeout catches YAML bombs. Neither the vm context nor the timeout prevents synchronous code execution from !!js/function evaluation or prototype pollution side effects.
fix_recommendation: Use yaml.load() with a restricted schema option: { schema: yaml.FAILSAFE_SCHEMA } or { schema: yaml.JSON_SCHEMA }. Upgrade to js-yaml 4.x minimum. Sanitize parsed object keys before any merge operations to reject prototype-polluting property names.

question: Q4
title: Hardcoded Alchemy API key in WebSocket provider URL — secret exposure
data_flow: Hardcoded Alchemy API key embedded in source code. The WebSocket provider URL is not user-controlled for the connection target, but req.body.walletAddress is stored in a Set without address format validation and matched against blockchain events.
exploit_scenario: The Alchemy API key 'FZDapFZSs1l6yhHW4VnQqsi18qSd-3GJ' is visible in source and version control. Any party with repo access can exhaust API quotas, monitor all Sepolia transactions under this key, or enumerate contract interactions. Separately, walletAddress from req.body is added to walletsConnected without format validation — any string value, including non-address garbage or injection payloads, is accepted and stored.
framework_protection: None for key exposure. The contract event listener requires the exploiter address to match blockchain event data, constraining the challenge solve path but not the API key abuse scenario.
fix_recommendation: Move API keys to environment variables (process.env.ALCHEMY_API_KEY). Rotate the exposed key immediately. Validate walletAddress against Ethereum address format (0x followed by exactly 40 hex characters) before storing in any collection.

question: Q5
title: Prototype pollution risk via unvalidated req.body.query used as username and object key
data_flow: req.body.query (arbitrary user string, setname action) -> UserModel.update({username: req.body.query}) -> bot.addUser(id, username). The raw query value is passed as a username to the bot library, which may use it as a plain object key internally.
exploit_scenario: Attacker sends action=setname with query='__proto__'. If the juicy-chat-bot library stores users in a plain object using bracket notation without hasOwnProperty guards, this poisons Object.prototype. Subsequent property lookups on any plain object in the process then return attacker-influenced values — potentially bypassing auth checks, flag logic, or object existence tests.
framework_protection: Sequelize's update() is parameterized and safe for the DB write. The prototype pollution risk exists in the downstream bot library's usage of the username string as an object map key. No input sanitization is applied to req.body.query before use as username.
fix_recommendation: Sanitize username input to reject reserved JS property names (__proto__, constructor, prototype). Use Map instead of plain objects for user registries inside bot libraries. Apply a username length and character whitelist before passing to bot.addUser().

question: Q6
title: Path traversal in ZIP upload extraction enables arbitrary file write
data_flow: Uploaded ZIP file -> unzipper.Parse() -> entry.path (attacker-controlled zip entry name) -> path.resolve() for guard check -> fs.createWriteStream('uploads/complaints/' + fileName) for actual write. The entry path from the ZIP archive header is used directly in path concatenation without normalization.
exploit_scenario: A ZIP with entry named '../../ftp/legal.md' — path.resolve('uploads/complaints/../../ftp/legal.md') resolves to cwd/ftp/legal.md, which contains path.resolve('.') (the cwd), passing the guard. The WriteStream is opened at 'uploads/complaints/../../ftp/legal.md', overwriting ftp/legal.md outside the intended directory. With deeper traversal any file writable by the Node process can be overwritten — enabling persistent code injection. The fileWriteChallenge confirms this traversal works in practice.
framework_protection: The absolutePath.includes(path.resolve('.')) check is bypassable: any traversal that stays within the cwd passes. The check prevents absolute-path escapes (e.g., /etc/passwd) but does not prevent relative traversal within the working directory tree.
fix_recommendation: Normalize with path.basename(entry.path) to strip all directory components, or validate strictly: ensure path.resolve('uploads/complaints/', safeFileName).startsWith(path.resolve('uploads/complaints') + path.sep) before opening WriteStream. Never concatenate zip entry paths directly into filesystem paths.

question: Q6
title: Open redirect with allowlist bypass enables client-side and chained server-side SSRF
data_flow: HTTP GET ?to=<user_input> -> security.isRedirectAllowed(toUrl) -> res.redirect(toUrl). The 'to' parameter is fully user-controlled. isUnintendedRedirect() uses utils.startsWith() for prefix matching against the allowlist — vulnerable to domain confusion attacks.
exploit_scenario: If allowlisted URLs include 'https://github.com/bkimminich', an attacker submits to=https://github.com/bkimminich.attacker.com. utils.startsWith() passes this as it begins with the allowlisted prefix. The redirectChallenge confirms a bypass path exists. In a chain: any server-side component that follows redirects (webhook processor, SSR renderer, PDF generator) would be directed to an attacker-controlled or internal target — constituting SSRF.
framework_protection: security.isRedirectAllowed() provides partial protection. The redirectChallenge confirms bypass is possible. The allowlist is not enforced using strict hostname comparison.
fix_recommendation: Replace startsWith prefix matching with strict URL parsing: use the URL constructor to parse both the target and each allowlisted URL, then compare the hostname property exactly. Reject any URL where the parsed hostname does not exactly equal an allowlisted hostname.
